The leaders of the tribe of Manasseh represent the evil that could follow if the daughters of Zelophehad were to marry into other tribes. They sought to preserve God’s plan for inheritances, so that arguments and disagreements would not arise among those who came later. It is the wisdom and duty of people who own property in the world to settle it and dispose of it, so that no strife or contention may arise.

Those who seek guidance from God concerning securing their heavenly inheritance will not only be directed what to do, but their requests will be graciously accepted. God would not have one tribe enriched at the expense of another. Each tribe was to keep to its own inheritance. The daughters of Zelophehad submitted to this plan. How could they fail to marry well when God himself directed them? Let the people of God learn how suitable and proper it is, like the daughters of Israel, to be united only to their own people. Ought not every true believer in Israel to be united only to their own people? Ought not every true believer in Jesus to be very attentive in close relationships, to be united only to those who are united to the Lord? All our intentions and desires ought to be subjected to God’s will when that is made known to us, especially when entering marriage. Although God's word allows affection and preference in this important relationship, it does not approve of foolish, uncontrolled, and idolatrous passion, which cares not what the outcome may be; but in defiance of authority, determines upon self-satisfaction. All such conduct, however disguised, is against common sense, the interests of society, the happiness of marriage, and, even more importantly, against the religion of Christ.

These are the judgments the Lord commanded in the plains of Moab. Most of them related to settling in Canaan, into which the Israelites were now entering. Whatever new situation God brings us into through his care, we must ask him to teach us our duties and enable us to do them, so that we may do the work of the day in its day, the duty of a place in its place.
